source.note: Unit 1 Grammar 1 (Future predictions and plans with be going to, planner pp.46-49) and Grammar 2 (Zero conditional present tense, planner pp.52-53). Grammar Workbook answer key (l5_ak-gwb.pdf pp.2-5) was available and used for authentic practice sentences.

grammarPoints.subject: english
grammarPoints.title: Future predictions and plans with be going to
grammarPoints.rule: Use be (am/is/are) + going to + the base form of the verb to make predictions about the future, especially when there's evidence something is likely to happen, and to talk about definite plans or intentions.
grammarPoints.pattern: subject + be (am/is/are) + going to + base verb

chart.title: Future predictions and plans with be going to

intro_examples.text: Is it going to rain tomorrow? No, it's going to snow tomorrow.
intro_examples.jp: 明日は雨が降りますか。いいえ、明日は雪が降ります。

intro_examples.text: I'm going to listen to the weather report at 8:00.
intro_examples.jp: 私は8時に天気予報を聞くつもりです。

intro_examples.text: He's going to put on his snow boots.
intro_examples.jp: 彼は雪用のブーツを履くつもりです。

rows.example: I'm going to make a snowman.
rows.jp: 私は雪だるまを作るつもりです。

rows.example: We're going to close all the windows.
rows.jp: 私たちは窓を全部閉めるつもりです。

rows.example: It's going to be sunny on Friday.
rows.jp: 金曜日は晴れるでしょう。

rows.pattern: subject + be + not + going to + verb
rows.example: I'm not going to play outside.
rows.jp: 私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。

chart.note_rule: Use be going to + the base form of the verb to make a prediction about the future, especially when there is evidence that something is likely to happen, or to talk about a definite plan.
chart.note_exception: In yes/no questions, the be verb comes before the subject: Is it going to rain today?
chart.note_exception_detail: For definite plans, only use be going to (or the present progressive) — not will. Reply to a yes/no question with a short answer (Yes, it is. / No, it isn't.), or say Maybe / I don't know if you're not sure.

table.title: be going to Table
notes: Contract be + going to in speech and informal writing: it's going to, I'm going to, we're going to, they're going to.
Make the negative by adding not after be: I'm not going to, it isn't going to.

tab1_samples.text: It's going to rain on Monday.
tab1_samples.jp: 月曜日は雨が降るでしょう。

tab1_samples.text: It's going to snow on Tuesday.
tab1_samples.jp: 火曜日は雪が降るでしょう。

tab1_samples.text: It's going to be windy on Wednesday.
tab1_samples.jp: 水曜日は風が強いでしょう。

tab1_samples.text: It's going to be cloudy on Thursday.
tab1_samples.jp: 木曜日は曇りでしょう。

tab1_samples.text: It's going to be sunny on Friday.
tab1_samples.jp: 金曜日は晴れるでしょう。

tab1_samples.text: I'm going to make a snowman.
tab1_samples.jp: 私は雪だるまを作るつもりです。

tab1_samples.text: I'm going to bring an umbrella.
tab1_samples.jp: 私は傘を持って行くつもりです。

tab1_samples.text: We're going to close all the windows.
tab1_samples.jp: 私たちは窓を全部閉めるつもりです。

tab1_samples.text: They're going to evacuate.
tab1_samples.jp: 彼らは避難するつもりです。

tab1_samples.text: I'm not going to play outside.
tab1_samples.jp: 私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。

rules.title: Use 's going to / 'm going to / 're going to for predictions
rules.jp_title: 予測には 's going to / 'm going to / 're going to を使う
rules.subtitle: Contract be + going to when you're confident about what's likely to happen, especially with evidence like a weather report or what you can see.
rules.jp_subtitle: 天気予報など根拠があって、これから起こりそうなことに確信があるときは be + going to を短縮形にします。

transforms.from: We / have a very cold winter
transforms.to: We're going to have a very cold winter.

examples.jp: 明日は雪が降るでしょう。

examples.text: We're going to have a very cold winter.
examples.jp: とても寒い冬になるでしょう。

rules.title: Use be going to for definite plans
rules.jp_title: 確定した予定には be going to を使う
rules.subtitle: Use be going to (not will) to talk about something you've already decided to do.
rules.jp_subtitle: すでに決めていることを話すときは will ではなく be going to を使います。

transforms.to: I'm going to wear shorts tomorrow.

transforms.from: My family / go skiing this weekend
transforms.to: My family is going to go skiing this weekend.

examples.text: I'm going to wear shorts tomorrow.
examples.jp: 私は明日ショートパンツを履くつもりです。

examples.text: My family is going to go skiing this weekend.
examples.jp: 私の家族は今週末スキーに行く予定です。

rules.title: Make the negative with be + not + going to
rules.jp_title: 否定文は be + not + going to で作る
rules.subtitle: Put not right after be to say what isn't going to happen.
rules.jp_subtitle: 起こらないことを言うときは be のすぐ後に not を置きます。

transforms.to: I'm not going to play outside.
It isn't going to be sunny today.

examples.text: I'm not going to play outside.
examples.jp: 私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。

examples.text: It isn't going to be sunny today.
examples.jp: 今日は晴れないでしょう。

rules.title: Ask yes/no questions by putting be before the subject
rules.jp_title: be を主語の前に置いて Yes/No 疑問文を作る
rules.subtitle: Move be to the front of the sentence to ask a yes/no question with going to; answer with a short answer or say Maybe if you're not sure.
rules.jp_subtitle: going to を使った Yes/No 疑問文では be を文の最初に置きます。確信が持てないときは Maybe と答えます。

transforms.to: Is it going to rain tomorrow?

examples.text: Is it going to rain tomorrow?
examples.jp: 明日は雨が降りますか。
彼らは避難する予定ですか。

mixed_samples.text: Tomorrow it's going to be hot.
mixed_samples.jp: 明日は暑くなるでしょう。

mixed_samples.text: Tomorrow I'm going to sleep late.
mixed_samples.jp: 明日私は遅くまで寝るつもりです。

mixed_samples.text: Tomorrow my brother is going to go to school.
mixed_samples.jp: 明日私の兄（弟）は学校に行きます。

mixed_samples.text: Tomorrow my parents are going to work.
mixed_samples.jp: 明日私の両親は仕事に行きます。

mixed_samples.text: Tomorrow it's going to be sunny.
mixed_samples.jp: 明日は晴れるでしょう。

mixed_samples.text: I'm going to evacuate if there's a flood.
mixed_samples.jp: 洪水が起きたら私は避難するつもりです。

mixed_samples.text: He's going to get a new raincoat today.
mixed_samples.jp: 彼は今日新しいレインコートを買うつもりです。

mixed_samples.text: It's going to snow a lot.
mixed_samples.jp: たくさん雪が降るでしょう。

mixed_samples.text: Is it going to rain much this week?
mixed_samples.jp: 今週はたくさん雨が降りますか。

mixed_samples.text: I'm not going to play outside if there's thunder and lightning.
mixed_samples.jp: 雷が鳴ったら私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。

stem: We just saw lightning. Next there

explanation.title: Match be to the subject
explanation.body: 'There' pairs with 's going to' (there is going to) when predicting something based on evidence like lightning.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雷を見たところです。次は雷鳴が鳴るでしょう。

stem: A sandstorm is coming. My parents

explanation.title: Plural subject takes are going to
explanation.body: 'My parents' is plural, so use 'are going to'.

tab3_quiz.jp: 砂嵐が来ています。両親は家の準備をするつもりです。

explanation.title: He takes 's going to
explanation.body: Third-person singular subjects (he/she/it) use 's going to.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雪が降っています。彼は暖かいコートを着るつもりです。

explanation.title: I takes 'm going to
explanation.body: The subject 'I' pairs with the contraction 'm going to.

tab3_quiz.jp: 今日は暑いです。私は泳ぎに行くつもりです。

stem: What are you going to do if it's rainy?

explanation.title: Keep be + going to + base verb together
explanation.body: The word order is subject + be + going to + base verb: I'm going to bring.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雨が降ったら何をするつもりですか。傘を持って行くつもりです。

stem: How is your family going to get ready for the sandstorm? We

explanation.title: We takes 're going to
explanation.body: The subject 'we' pairs with the contraction 're going to.

tab3_quiz.jp: 砂嵐にどう備えますか。私たちは窓を全部閉めるつもりです。

stem: What are your cousins going to do if there's a flood? They

explanation.title: They takes 're going to
explanation.body: The subject 'they' pairs with the contraction 're going to.

tab3_quiz.jp: 洪水が起きたらいとこたちは何をしますか。避難するつもりです。

stem: What are you not going to do if there's thunder and lightning?

explanation.title: Put not right after be
explanation.body: The negative goes subject + be + not + going to + base verb: I'm not going to play.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雷が鳴ったら何をしないつもりですか。外で遊ばないつもりです。

explanation.title: Move be before the subject
explanation.body: Yes/no questions with going to put be first: Is it going to...?

tab3_quiz.jp: 明日は雨が降りますか。

stem: If you have to evacuate your house, where

explanation.title: Wh-question word order
explanation.body: Wh-word + be + subject + going to: where are you going to go?

tab3_quiz.jp: 家から避難しなければならないとき、どこへ行くつもりですか。

stem: Which sentence correctly predicts the weather?

explanation.title: Correct be going to word order
explanation.body: Use subject + be + going to + base verb: It's going to rain tomorrow.

tab4_master.jp: 明日は雨が降るでしょう。

answers: I'm not going to play outside.
I amn't going to play outside.
I'm going to not play outside.
I not going to play outside.

explanation.title: Negative word order
explanation.body: Not goes right after be: I'm not going to play outside.

tab4_master.jp: 私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。

answers: Is it going to rain tomorrow?
It is going to rain tomorrow?
Is going to it rain tomorrow?

explanation.title: Question word order
explanation.body: Move be before the subject: Is it going to rain tomorrow?

tab4_master.jp: 明日は雨が降りますか。

explanation.title: Plural/you subjects take 're
explanation.body: We, you, and they use 're going to; he, she, it use 's going to.

tab4_master.jp: 「We」が 're going to と一致します。

stem: What's the base form of the verb after going to in 'It's going to snow'?

explanation.title: Always use the base verb
explanation.body: After going to, use the base form of the verb with no -s, -ing, or -ed.

tab4_master.jp: going to の後は動詞の原形を使います。
水曜日は風が強いでしょう。
私たちは窓を全部閉めるつもりです。
私は外で遊ぶつもりはありません。
明日は雨が降りますか。
彼らは避難するつもりです。

japanese.title: be going to を使った未来の予測と予定
japanese.rule: be動詞（am/is/are）+ going to + 動詞の原形を使って、これから起こりそうなことを予測したり、確定した予定や意図を話したりします。
japanese.pattern: 主語 + be動詞（am/is/are）+ going to + 動詞の原形

examples.sentence: It's going to rain on Monday.
It's going to snow on Tuesday.
It's going to be windy on Wednesday.
It's going to be cloudy on Thursday.
It's going to be sunny on Friday.
I'm going to make a snowman.
I'm going to bring an umbrella.
We're going to close all the windows.
They're going to evacuate.
I'm not going to play outside.
Tomorrow it's going to be hot.
Tomorrow I'm going to sleep late.
Tomorrow my brother is going to go to school.
Tomorrow my parents are going to work.
Tomorrow it's going to be sunny.
I'm going to evacuate if there's a flood.
He's going to get a new raincoat today.
It's going to snow a lot.
Is it going to rain much this week?
I'm not going to play outside if there's thunder and lightning.

source.note: Grammar Workbook pp. 2-3 (Level 5 Unit 1) answer key used for authentic practice sentences alongside the planner grammar box and Be the Expert notes.

grammarPoints.subject: english
grammarPoints.title: Zero conditional (present tense)
grammarPoints.rule: Use If + simple present in the if-clause with simple present in the result clause to talk about things that are generally true or always happen under certain conditions. The clauses can be reversed without changing the meaning; use a comma only when the if-clause comes first.
grammarPoints.pattern: If + subject + present verb, subject + present verb

chart.title: Zero conditional (present tense)

intro_examples.text: I put on my winter coat if the weather is cold.
intro_examples.jp: 寒いときは冬用のコートを着ます。

intro_examples.text: If I see lightning, I go inside.
intro_examples.jp: 雷を見たら中に入ります。

intro_examples.text: If a sandstorm comes, I close all the windows.
intro_examples.jp: 砂嵐が来たら窓を全部閉めます。

rows.example: If it snows, school starts late.
rows.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

rows.example: School starts late if it snows.
rows.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります（語順を入れ替えても同じ意味）。

rows.pattern: If + subject + be + adjective, subject + verb
rows.example: If the weather is cold, I put on my coat.
rows.jp: 天気が寒いときは、コートを着ます。

rows.example: If there's a blizzard, we don't have school.
rows.jp: 吹雪になったら学校は休みです。

chart.note_rule: Both the if-clause and the result clause use the simple present, even though the sentence talks about a general truth or repeated situation, not just now.
chart.note_exception: You can reverse the order of the clauses without changing the meaning.
chart.note_exception_detail: Use a comma after the if-clause only when it comes first: If it snows, school starts late. But: School starts late if it snows. (no comma)

table.title: Zero Conditional Table
notes: Use a comma after the if-clause when it comes first in the sentence.
Both clauses use the simple present tense, even for future or repeated conditions.

tab1_samples.text: If it snows, school starts late.
tab1_samples.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

tab1_samples.text: If I see lightning, I go inside.
tab1_samples.jp: 雷を見たら中に入ります。

tab1_samples.text: If a sandstorm comes, I close all the windows.
tab1_samples.jp: 砂嵐が来たら窓を全部閉めます。

tab1_samples.text: If it rains, I use an umbrella.
tab1_samples.jp: 雨が降ったら傘を使います。

tab1_samples.text: If a tornado comes, we evacuate.
tab1_samples.jp: 竜巻が来たら私たちは避難します。

tab1_samples.text: If Jon knows it's going to be cold, he wears his coat and gloves.
tab1_samples.jp: ジョンは寒くなるとわかったら、コートと手袋を身につけます。

tab1_samples.text: If it's hot, we go swimming.
tab1_samples.jp: 暑いときは泳ぎに行きます。

tab1_samples.text: If there's a blizzard, we don't have school.
tab1_samples.jp: 吹雪になったら学校は休みです。

tab1_samples.text: If the electricity goes out, we use a flashlight.
tab1_samples.jp: 停電したら懐中電灯を使います。

tab1_samples.text: If there's a flood in my town, people evacuate.
tab1_samples.jp: 私の町で洪水が起きたら、人々は避難します。

rules.title: Put if before the condition
rules.jp_title: 条件の前に if を置く
rules.subtitle: Start the sentence with If + the condition, then add a comma before the result.
rules.jp_subtitle: 文を If + 条件 で始め、結果の前にコンマを入れます。

transforms.from: it / rain / I / use an umbrella
transforms.to: If it rains, I use an umbrella.

transforms.from: a storm / come / we / go inside
transforms.to: If a storm comes, we go inside.

examples.text: If it rains, I use an umbrella.
examples.jp: 雨が降ったら傘を使います。

examples.text: If a storm comes, we go inside.
examples.jp: 嵐が来たら私たちは中に入ります。

rules.title: Reverse the clauses without a comma
rules.jp_title: コンマなしで節を入れ替える
rules.subtitle: Put the result first and drop the comma; the meaning stays the same.
rules.jp_subtitle: 結果を先に置き、コンマを省きます。意味は変わりません。

transforms.from: If it snows, school starts late.
transforms.to: School starts late if it snows.

transforms.from: If the weather is cold, I put on my coat.
transforms.to: I put on my coat if the weather is cold.

examples.text: School starts late if it snows.
examples.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

examples.text: I put on my coat if the weather is cold.
examples.jp: 寒いときはコートを着ます。

rules.title: Use be in the if-clause for conditions, not actions
rules.jp_title: 動作でなく状態の条件には if 節で be動詞を使う
rules.subtitle: When the condition is a state (not something that happens), use be + adjective after if.
rules.jp_subtitle: 条件が（出来事ではなく）状態のときは、if の後に be + 形容詞を使います。

transforms.from: the temperature / rise / I / try to stay cool
transforms.to: If the temperature rises, I try to stay cool.

transforms.from: it / be hot / we / go swimming
transforms.to: If it's hot, we go swimming.

examples.text: If the temperature rises, I try to stay cool.
examples.jp: 気温が上がったら涼しくしようとします。

examples.text: If it's hot, we go swimming.
examples.jp: 暑いときは泳ぎに行きます。

rules.title: Use there's/there are for conditions about existence
rules.jp_title: 存在を表す条件には there's/there are を使う
rules.subtitle: Use If there's / If there are to talk about something existing or happening, like weather events.
rules.jp_subtitle: 天候の出来事など、何かが存在したり起こったりすることを言うときは If there's / If there are を使います。

transforms.to: If there's a drought, farmers need more water.

transforms.from: there / be no rain for weeks
transforms.to: If there's no rain for weeks, there's a drought.

examples.text: If there's a drought, farmers need more water.
examples.jp: 干ばつが起きたら、農家はもっと水が必要です。

examples.text: If there's no rain for weeks, there's a drought.
examples.jp: 何週間も雨が降らなければ、干ばつになります。

mixed_samples.text: If there's an emergency, we follow our plan.
mixed_samples.jp: 緊急事態が起きたら、私たちは計画に従います。

mixed_samples.text: If we need to evacuate, we go to a storm shelter.
mixed_samples.jp: 避難が必要なら、私たちは避難所に行きます。

mixed_samples.text: If there's no electricity, we need a flashlight.
mixed_samples.jp: 電気がなければ、懐中電灯が必要です。

mixed_samples.text: If there's a bad storm, I make an emergency plan.
mixed_samples.jp: ひどい嵐が来たら、私は緊急計画を立てます。

mixed_samples.text: If I'm late, I call my parents.
mixed_samples.jp: 遅れるときは両親に電話します。

mixed_samples.text: If it rains, people wear rain boots.
mixed_samples.jp: 雨が降ったら、人々はレインブーツを履きます。

mixed_samples.text: I play outside with my friends if it snows.
mixed_samples.jp: 雪が降ったら友達と外で遊びます。

mixed_samples.text: Dogs drink a lot of water if there's a heat wave.
mixed_samples.jp: 熱波が来たら、犬はたくさん水を飲みます。

mixed_samples.text: If I see a lion, I run.
mixed_samples.jp: ライオンを見たら、私は逃げます。

mixed_samples.text: If a flood comes, people evacuate.
mixed_samples.jp: 洪水が来たら、人々は避難します。

explanation.title: Use simple present in both clauses
explanation.body: Zero conditional uses simple present in the if-clause and the result clause: If it snows, school starts late.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

explanation.title: Simple present in the result clause
explanation.body: The result clause also uses the simple present: I go inside.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雷を見たら中に入ります。

explanation.title: Third person singular -s
explanation.body: 'A sandstorm' is singular, so the verb takes -s: comes.

tab3_quiz.jp: 砂嵐が来たら窓を全部閉めます。

stem: If Jon knows it's going to be cold, he

explanation.title: Third person singular -s
explanation.body: 'He' takes the -s form in the simple present: wears.

tab3_quiz.jp: ジョンは寒くなるとわかったら、コートと手袋を身につけます。

explanation.title: Start the if-clause with If
explanation.body: Zero conditional sentences begin the condition with If.

tab3_quiz.jp: 暑いときは泳ぎに行きます。

explanation.title: Negative simple present with we
explanation.body: 'We' takes 'don't' in the simple present negative.

tab3_quiz.jp: 吹雪になったら学校は休みです。

stem: If the electricity goes out, we

explanation.title: Simple present in the result clause
explanation.body: The result clause uses the simple present: we use.

tab3_quiz.jp: 停電したら懐中電灯を使います。

explanation.title: No comma when the result clause comes first
explanation.body: When the if-clause comes second, there is no comma before it.

tab3_quiz.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

stem: If there's a flood in my town, people

explanation.title: Simple present with plural subject
explanation.body: 'People' is plural, so use the base form: evacuate.

tab3_quiz.jp: 私の町で洪水が起きたら、人々は避難します。

explanation.title: Simple present with I
explanation.body: 'I' takes the base form in the simple present: see.

tab3_quiz.jp: ライオンを見たら、私は逃げます。

stem: Which sentence correctly uses the zero conditional?
answers: If it snows, school starts late.
If it snows, school will start late.
If it will snow, school starts late.
If it snowing, school starts late.

explanation.title: Simple present in both clauses
explanation.body: The zero conditional uses the simple present in both the if-clause and the result clause.

tab4_master.jp: 雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。

answers: If there's a flood, people evacuate.
People evacuate if there's a flood.
People evacuate, if there's a flood.
If, there's a flood people evacuate.

explanation.title: Comma only when if-clause comes first
explanation.body: Use a comma after the if-clause only when it comes first in the sentence.

tab4_master.jp: 洪水が来たら、人々は避難します。

answers: If the weather is cold, I put on my coat.
If the weather cold, I put on my coat.
If the weather being cold, I put on my coat.
If the weather was cold, I put on my coat.

explanation.title: Use be + adjective for states
explanation.body: Describe a state with be + adjective: the weather is cold.

tab4_master.jp: 天気が寒いときは、コートを着ます。

stem: Which sentence reverses the clauses correctly?
answers: I put on my coat if the weather is cold.
I put on my coat, if the weather is cold.
If I put on my coat the weather is cold.
I put on my coat if the weather cold.

explanation.title: No comma when result clause is first
explanation.body: When the result clause comes first, drop the comma before if.

tab4_master.jp: 寒いときはコートを着ます。

stem: What tense do both clauses use in the zero conditional?

explanation.title: Simple present, not future
explanation.body: Even though the sentence can describe the future, both clauses stay in the simple present.

tab4_master.jp: ゼロ条件文は両方の節で現在形を使います。
雨が降ったら傘を使います。
竜巻が来たら私たちは避難します。
雪が降ったら学校は遅く始まります。
吹雪になったら学校は休みです。
ライオンを見たら、私は逃げます。

japanese.title: ゼロ条件文（現在形）
japanese.rule: if節と結果節の両方に現在形を使って、一般的に真実であることや、ある条件のもとでいつも起こることを表します。節の順序は入れ替え可能で、if節が先に来るときだけコンマを使います。
japanese.pattern: If + 主語 + 現在形の動詞, 主語 + 現在形の動詞

examples.sentence: If it snows, school starts late.
If I see lightning, I go inside.
If a sandstorm comes, I close all the windows.
If it rains, I use an umbrella.
If a tornado comes, we evacuate.
If Jon knows it's going to be cold, he wears his coat and gloves.
examples.highlight: If Jon knows it's going to be cold

examples.sentence: If it's hot, we go swimming.
If there's a blizzard, we don't have school.
If the electricity goes out, we use a flashlight.
If there's a flood in my town, people evacuate.
examples.highlight: If there's a flood in my town

examples.sentence: If there's an emergency, we follow our plan.
If we need to evacuate, we go to a storm shelter.
If there's no electricity, we need a flashlight.
If there's a bad storm, I make an emergency plan.
If I'm late, I call my parents.
If it rains, people wear rain boots.
I play outside with my friends if it snows.
Dogs drink a lot of water if there's a heat wave.
If I see a lion, I run.
If a flood comes, people evacuate.

source.note: Grammar Workbook pp. 4-5 (Level 5 Unit 1) answer key used for authentic practice sentences alongside the planner grammar box and Be the Expert notes.
